speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interviewing Reverend Ernest Levy. This is Tape number 3. Your first day at Auschwitz, could you please continue about when you went to the block and people you met there and your illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was total exhaustion. As I threw myself down on a bunk bed, excuse me, when I woke up, there were three, four people lying on top of me. And still no toilets, no water, no food, no nothing. We were ankle deep in urine. In spite of that, there was a group of people actually started praying. Unbelievable. And then we woke up and we looked up. There were no windows in the block, but there were grills on top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we looked up. And we saw prisoners pushing their faces against the grills and speaking in Yiddish to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What did they say? [SPEAKING YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: It will make no difference. Praying will not help you. You will all end up in the chimneys. That was the moment of truth for us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And I was in agony, needless to say, and no food, no water. Eventually, we got some kind of soup the second day. And the first days in Auschwitz were in a maze. We had to pose for photos, constantly posing for photos for the German-- for the Nazis. They went around making photos. Right. That was the first days in Auschwitz. But what was the worst about Auschwitz, as far as I am concerned, the stench, the smell of the burning flesh. I just could not breathe. Just very shallow breath as I could take. I couldn't breathe. It was tearing my lungs apart. I suffered hell. I couldn't understand how people could function there, how people could live there. It was lying on-- on my-- on my lungs, tearing me apart. I was suffering hell. I couldn't breathe, just 'huh.' That was the most terrible thing in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the significance of the stench?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, by then. By then we knew. As we heard from the Polish prisoners, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. We knew already. We knew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the people in your block the people who came with you from Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Correct. Correct. But, you were-- you were in a maze. You didn't know what's what's going on, what's-- For the people of Auschwitz, for the guards and for the kapos, that was a routine already. But for us, remember, to find yourself in a world of-- of evilness, of-- of madness beyond any human imagination. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a few days, we were selected for work. And I remember I found myself again in a goods train. I was kissing the walls of the goods train hysterically, just for every yard the train took me away from that place. For every yard, I was counting my blessings saying [NON-ENGLISH  SPEECH], kissing the walls of that dirty goods train. I got away from that stench. So that was my career, so to speak, in Auschwitz. During your few days' stay in Auschwitz, did you hear anything about the-- how they-- how they operated the mass destruction of human beings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know the details. I never saw the crematoriums close-- from close or so. No. We were too-- too short time in Auschwitz for that. So we were taken away without ever seeing the crematoriums. We were chosen for life. We were selected for life and for labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people who took photographs of you, German officers, did you ever see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Never. No. I saw many of them right in that part of the camp going around with the cameras enjoying themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: During those few days, what happened to your physical disability of not being able to relieve yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just did not want to work. It just didn't want to work. The agony is undescribable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So then when we were arriving in the new camp, which was a sister camp of Gross-Rosen, Wustegiersdorf, a few days later, I was still in trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: The journey took a few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: The journey took less-- less-- a few hours, I would say, a few hours. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the treatment on that journey? Food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. Yes, we got some food. Before we-- before we-- before we entered the-- the train, we got some food from the Germans, because remember, we were selected for work, so we had to get some minimum food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your companions and you aware that your life had been spared for the time being?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We were aware of that, because we never saw the rest of the people again. We were aware that we had been selected for work. And that was good news, and away from Auschwitz. And we were lucky. We didn't get to Gross-Rosen, because Gross-Rosen was an absolute hell. The greatest threat for anybody was you could end up in Gross-Rosen. If a German told you you'd end up in Gross-Rosen, that was the greatest threat. That was absolute hell, Gross-Rosen. So we were lucky. We ended up in a little camp between Auschwitz and Gross-Rosen where I spent nearly 10 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Gross-Rosen an extermination camp or a labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A labor camp. But the kapos had full, full power in hand. People were beaten to death daily and-- and dozens of people there, beaten to death daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your arrival at Wustegiersdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. I do. There was a large factory building, where they stupidly threw out all the beautiful machinery to make place for these unfortunate people. I couldn't believe it. The whole surrounding of that factory was littered with the most beautiful brass and copper machinery. I mean, I knew a little bit about machinery. I couldn't-- to make place for these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unfortunate, displaced people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we went into that factory building, which was converted into a concentration camp. And there were Polish prisoners there, 2/3 of them the Polish prisoners. And we were the Hungarian prisoners. We were housed on top of the factory building. And the Polish prisoners were the first and second floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a lot of tension and animosity between the Hungarian and Polish prisoners, because they felt they are here already, I don't know since when. You know how it is. We are the newcomers. We have to suffer. I mean, many people were already humiliated--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dehumanized.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dehumanized by that time. Right? And so there was problems. But eventually, we settled to a life of very little food, little drink. Our commander, Schwarz, relatively speaking-- a man from Dusseldorf, an enormous big chap, the commander of the camp. Relatively speaking, not the worst type.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our life settled for something acceptable, let's call it, no more. And there was food. There was a little discipline.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, I ended up in a little factory, which was my life-saver. There met another Schindler, a man from the Organisation Todt, a little man. His name was Anton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain what Organisation Todt is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Organisation Todt is the industrial-- they ran the industry for the war efforts. That was the Organisation Todt. Also in uniform, in brown uniform wearing the big swastika, right? A Nazi-- a Nazi uniform. And affiliated to the army, looking after the-- the financial-- not financial, the-- the economical part of the army and the industrial part. So that was the Organisation Todt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's just go back to Schwarz? Was he an SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: An SS officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a sturmbannfuhrer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sturmbannfuhrer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did you have any contact with him? Did you meet  him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had. I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say something about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had a little [INAUDIBLE] with him one morning, because Anton, our Organisation Todt officer in the factory, was a tremendous, fantastic human being. He fought for us, for food, that we worked, we need to be fed. And it's called-- we called his idea the Anton's pipes, because he found out one day how could we-- how could we smuggle food in from the factory into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he got hold of some stove-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stove pipes. And he filled the bottom in with some rags. And we put the food into the pipes. And they gave some rags on top of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I marched in two, three times a week from that factory to the camp with food in the pipes. And with a lot of small letter of my-- asked if I could go do a repair job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, I met Schwarz. He says to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I said to him in German, there is some repair job to be done. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. How do you speak so well in German? [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: But he spoke to me like to a human being. I was so surprised. I said, I come from-- coming from Budapest. I come from-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And he gave a little smile. And he said, [GERMAN]. And he sent me away. That was the only time when I had really kind of personal contact with Schwarz. He could-- there were times when he was very, very-- very vicious as well. A little bit of a Jekyll and Hyde he was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] especially when he was watched by other officers. You see, the usual-- the usual pattern.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were at this labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1,000. 1,000 people, approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Schwarz was in charge of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schwarz was the commander, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know Anton's other name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, unfortunately. It's my greatest regret that I hadn't had the presence of mind to ask the name. I would have loved to have thanked that man after the war to say to him, thank you, Anton, if he was-- if he survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what rank he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. I couldn't really tell  you what rank he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he decent to all the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Decent is an understatement. He fought for us. There was-- half of us, we had no factory experience. And he pretended that we were all vital for that war-- war effort. Vital we are. And he protected us. It was fantastic. A fantastic, little man, small, little stocky man. A wonderful human being, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until another job came. And then hell broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How How long was Anton there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time, actually, most of the-- the last few weeks, we had an elderly Organisation Todt officer, an absolute mad, mad, vicious beast.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the work you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did-- I worked actually on a lathe, because I had experience working on  a lathe. Repair jobs on vehicles, on trucks, on cars, on motorbikes. Yes. It was-- it was an important part, actually, for that factory to function.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wustegiersdorf is in Germany itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, yes. I would say not far from Breslau. Not far from Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the camp living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a Jewish Lageralteste Messinger, who was greatly respected by the Germans. Also an enormous big man from Bratslava, footballer, a famous footballer Messinger. And the Germans respected him, because of the liaison between the camp and-- and that made our life bearable, that man, who got-- after the war, he was sentenced to nine years of imprisonment, Messinger. And when we heard about the sentence, we got together and we testified for him, not against him. They reduced his-- they reduced his sentence to three years. Certainly, there were people who suffered from him, greatly. And that's how he got eventually nine years. But we reduced-- our-- our testimony, reduced his sentence to three years. And then we heard eventually that he was released after a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was no rank. He was a prisoner, a Jewish prisoner. But he was top man, so to speak, a lageralteste, the top man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any occasion-- did you have contact with kapos while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any actions--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --which you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. We had some terrible, horrifying kapos in Wustegiersdorf. Yanek, for instance, Yanek, who was the second in charge to Messinger. Yanek, ah, an absolute wild animal. Oh. Oh, my   god-- frightened even to look at him. A Jewish man, a Jewish prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then there were other ranks, right? I once lined up for soup a second time, which was one of the greatest crimes you can commit in a concentration camp, to go again to line-- to queue up for another. But what happened actually was in my turn-- when it was my turn, the little kapo put the ladle into the soup not deep enough. And half-- he gave me half a ladle of soup. I was devastated. I was crying inside, why me? Everybody got the full ladle of-- that was my lifeline, that soup. He give me half a ladle of soup. I was crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my decision was-- I took the wrong decision. Line up again. He will-- how can he recognize-- how-- you know, my pulse was going like that. I was so excited. God. I lined up. He put the ladle into the soup. And he looked up. You wouldn't believe it. He recognized me. He says, stand to the side. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My god. He called over other kapos. I got a terrible beating. But I was saved by a Hungarian kapo, Pinter. His name was Pinter, who was a very popular man in the camp. And he pulled me away from these people. I wouldn't be sitting here otherwise. They would have killed me there. He tore me away from the people. Don't touch that man. That's the best-behaved prisoner in the whole camp. I vouch for that man. Let me just deal with him. And so I survived that queuing up for another plate of soup. It gives you insight of-- of the life of a concentration camp, of a good concentration camp, a-- where things were quite normal, I mean, if the word is appropriate for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other beatings by kapos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure there was. Every morning, practically, the flogging and beatings by kapos. A lot of beatings were done by Messinger. And there was one morning when he was beating a young 16-, 17-year-old And he held back. He held back. It was obvious he was holding back. He was not beating. And Schwarz got hold of Messinger. If you don't beat-- you see, I can't-- I notice that you are not beating properly. You will get it unless you start beating properly. And still, Messinger did not beat properly that young boy. But certainly, when other kapos did the flogging, then it was terrible. There was hanging in the camp, hanging. If somebody tried to run away, which was impossible, but just the attempt, right, or  moved out, he got a big, red circle on his back on the pajama. And that was-- meant he is condemned to death, to hanging. And that's how sometimes weeks went by. And he knew he was going to hanged. And he was amongst us. Unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Schwarz-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was one hanging on Sunday evening when we all have to lined up. Again, a young Polish boy was hanged. Why? We were working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And a woman, a German woman far away on the pavement, the other side of the road, was holding a red apple. And she wanted to give some-- some of us. She wanted to be good. And that boy made for the apple. And it was, halt! Wait! He was pointing the rifle. And the boy, the boy turned back. Although the boy turned back, he was reported he wanted to run away. And so he was hanged, a 16-year-old boy. And the father had to watch, stand in front of the gallow, had to watch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Schwarz said to the father [SPEAKING  GERMAN]. Look at your offspring. Ooh, I was really deeply disappointed in Schwarz. That was so unnecessary. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Because why? Because the rest of the Nazi officers looked on. And then came the greatest tragedy in the world. There was nobody to kick the little stool out-- out from below-- from-- right? Nobody. And the Germans didn't. They wanted us to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Messinger said, no. Hang me, but I am not going. Messinger stood the ground-- stood his ground. It was the greatest tragedy you can imagine, standing there. And every fifth man will be shot now, if within five minutes there is nobody to-- I can't tell you these few minutes. And then a little Polish chap, he ran forward and kicked away the stool. I don't want to go into more details, because this-- it just wasn't properly done. And it was a long, long suffering. So that was a good camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How was, at that time, your illness with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Water.

speaker: interviewee
text: That water was OK. By then, it was OK, yes, because on the first day in Oberwustegiersdorf-- We went from Wustegiersdorf the first day out working on the railway, the line which was bombed. And there, I was beaten up by a young soldier, but to that extent that I thought every bone was broken. So I ended up in a lazaret, where under a shower, it started going. So that was my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: That beating took place early on.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Oberwustegiersdorf At the railway station, yes. But that soldier was killed eventually by one of us. We had a wrestler, a Mr. Braun from Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: One-- I didn't know, because I was in the lazaret. They came telling me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that Braun killed the guard. And we buried him among all the timber. He will never be found. That-- they came telling me to the lazaret. So that's the story of Oberwustegiersdorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lazaret was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Emergency hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a kind of hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: At-- at the-- was the guard German?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German guard, a pale-looking, young nobody. Just the uniform held him together, But give him power and give him a uniform-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was pleading with him, I can't work. I'm in such pain, I can't straighten up. And then he started beating me that I don't want to work, blah, blah, blah, cursing me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Braun from Budapest-- he beat him to death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. From behind. I was told one, one stroke was enough. It was getting darker. And that was the moment Braun-- because he kept kicking, beating people, spitting at people for no reason at all, an absolute young, little nobody and sadist, right, the produce of a Nazi regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was outside the-- the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside the camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Wustegiersdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: At labor-- labor-- forced labor. We called it forced labor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of-- Was there only soup food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In Wustegiersdorf, we got half-- half a slice of bread, a little margarine, and in the evening when we came home from work, a plate of soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the sanitary conditions in the living quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-existent. I mean, we had to use pails, a pail, right? And there were-- there were turns. You had to take your turn that when the pail was full up, you had to carry-- carry down the pail three floors and empty the pail and go  back, right? That was-- that-- and for your turn, for an hour you had to watch the pail.

speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived there on the 20th of April, 1944. Do you remember how long you were at Wustegiersdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say around 10 months, between 9 and 10 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did conditions improve or get worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Conditions improved. They did improve, first-- especially for me, who ended up in that workshop, that I was-- I was protected from the last winter of the war, which was pretty hard, because we had no adequate clothing for the winter. And there was extra food from Anton, right? And you felt a little-- again, you're a  mensch. Under Anton, you felt you are a mensch really truly. That was the real Schindler, because Schindler didn't do it really out of goodness, let's say. We don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that man Anton out of sheer goodness and [NON-ENGLISH], of human feelings that little man Anton. Never asked his second name. Why would I ask his second name? Anton is Anton finish. But how can you-- how can you thank you when everything is over if you don't know the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my greatest regret, not only for Anton, because there were other people who helped. Oh, yes, there were other people who showed some human feelings. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But look, the little girl, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anne Frank, she had the presence of mind to write down things. And through that little Anne Frank, we-- we got to know the enormity of the crime, right? But we hadn't had the presence of mind to write it down, make sure [INAUDIBLE]. In case you survive, there is-- you know, you remember. So that's a-- my greatest regret. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The winter of 1944, Wustegiersdorf, what happened then? How long did that go on?

words.text: How long did that go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the Russian Army, the Red Army, were advancing from the east. We could hear the bombardment already. So the-- the camp had to be evacuated. But it's so-- so typical how history is repeating itself. We had another lageralteste, Moses from Warsaw, a fantastic man. Fantastic man, also a Jewish prisoner. But somehow the Germans accepted him somehow. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came warning us that we are going to leave from Wustegiersdorf shortly, save up a little food. We didn't believe him like Moshe Rabin when he went to [INAUDIBLE]. Yes? They didn't believe [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: They came. And Moses, his name was Moses, he said save, and we didn't believe him, except my cousin Poshevy Lowy. He saved up food. And five minutes before he left, he put food in my pockets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Poshevy Lowy one of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: -- cousin from Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I came with Poshevy Lowy from Budapest to Wustegiersdorf.
